coredata.dc:title: Graphene-quantum-dots-decorated NiAl2O4 nanostructure as supercapacitor and electrocatalyst in biosensing
coredata.dc:creator: Regulska, Elzbieta
Breczko, Joanna
Basa, Anna
Szydlowska, Beata
Kakareko, Katarzyna
Rydzewska-Rosołowska, Alicja
Hryszko, Tomasz
coredata.dc:description: Nickel aluminate-graphene quantum dots (NiAl2O4/GQDs) composite is a nanostructured material which offers synergistic behavior and multifunctionality. The high porosity, biocompatibility, low-cost preparation and electrocatalytic performance of spinel nickel aluminate has been merged with high electrical conductivity of graphene quantum dots. The NiAl2O4/GQDs composite was characterized through SEM, TEM, XPS, EDX, IR techniques and porosimetry. The cyclic voltammetry, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and galvanostatic charge/discharge measurements allowed to investigate the electrochemical performance. The composite shows enhanced supercapacitive and electrocatalytic performance with respect to the pristine NiAl2O4. The NiAl2O4/GQDs-modified gold electrode has been applied to develop an electrochemical method for glucose detection. The constructed biosensor allowed to sense glucose in serum of diabetes patients.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanostructured materials
Graphene quantum dots
Spinel
Electrocatalyst
Biosensing
Diabetes mellitus

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Operational Programme Development of Eastern Poland
Polish Ministry of Science and Higher Education

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Polish Ministry of Science and Higher Education under subsidy for maintaining the research potential of the Faculty of Chemistry, Polish Ministry of Science and Higher Education . Diffractometer, IR spectrometer, SEM and TEM microscopes, porosimeter and potentiostat were funded by EU, as part of the Operational Programme Development of Eastern Poland , projects Nr POPW.01.03.00-20-034/09-00 and POPW.01.03.00-20-004/11-00 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a) NiAl2O4, (b) GQDs, and (c) NiAl2O4/GQDs. SAED patterns of (d) NiAl2O4 and (e) NiAl2O4/GQDs. (f) SEM images of NiAl2O4/GQDs. SEM-EDX mapping of (g) Ni), (h) Al, (i) O and (j) C. XPS spectra of (k-m) NiAl2O4 and (n-p) NiAl2O4/GQDs.
(a) IR spectra of NiAl2O4, GQDs, and NiAl2O4/GQDs. (b) N2 Adsorption-desorption isotherms of NiAl2O4, GQDs, and NiAl2O4/GQDs. (c) Pore size distribution profile for NiAl2O4, GQDs, and NiAl2O4/GQDs.

(a) CVs at bare and NiAl2O4/GQDs, NiAl2O4, and GQDs-modified Au electrodes (scan rate 100 mV s−1, 0.1 M NaOH). (b) EIS spectra at NiAl2O4/GQDs, NiAl2O4, and GQDs-modified Au electrodes in 0.1 M NaOH at 0.6 V. (c) Zoomed in EIS spectra from (b) and equivalent electrical circuit representing behavior of NiAl2O4/GQDs. (d) GCD curves of NiAl2O4/GQDs at different current densities. (e) Specific capacitance as a function of current density. (f) Ragone plot for the NiAl2O4/GQDs composite.

(a) CVs at different scan rates (5–200 mV s−1) at NiAl2O4/GQDs-modified Au electrode. (b) Plot of current vs. square root of scan rate at NiAl2O4/GQDs-modified Au electrode. (c) Differential pulse voltammograms at NiAl2O4/GQD-modified Au electrode upon increasing concentration of glucose. (d) Calibration curve at NiAl2O4/GQDs modified electrode in 0.1 M NaOH and varying concentration of glucose. (e) Amperometric responses to successive injections of 2 mM of glucose and interferents: ascorbic acid (AA), dopamine (DA), uric acid (UA) and the subsequent injection of 6 mM of glucose at NiAl2O4/GQDs-modified Au electrode in 0.1 M NaOH at the applied voltage of + 0.37 V.
The BET surface area, pore diameter, pore volume and specific capacitance of GQDs, NiAl2O4 and the NiAl2O4/GQDs.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: [a] Surface area calculated based on Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) isotherm. [b] Pore volume calculated based on Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) isotherm. [c] Specific capacitance derived from the CV studies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparative profile of various Ni-based electrochemical glucose sensors.
Glucose concentration determined in serum of diabetes patients (P1-P4) and control group of healthy people (C1-C4) with GQDs/NiAl2O4-modified electrode and a reference method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Patients at the age of 37, 65, 28 and 77, respectively, with diabetes type 2, except for P3 with diabetes type 1.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Glucose determined in the hospital using enzymatic method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Healthy people at the age of 35, 74, 39 and 35, respectively.

head.ce:title: Graphene-quantum-dots-decorated NiAl2O4 nanostructure as supercapacitor and electrocatalyst in biosensing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nickel aluminate-graphene quantum dots (NiAl2O4/GQDs) composite is a nanostructured material which offers synergistic behavior and multifunctionality. The high porosity, biocompatibility, low-cost preparation and electrocatalytic performance of spinel nickel aluminate has been merged with high electrical conductivity of graphene quantum dots. The NiAl2O4/GQDs composite was characterized through SEM, TEM, XPS, EDX, IR techniques and porosimetry. The cyclic voltammetry, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and galvanostatic charge/discharge measurements allowed to investigate the electrochemical performance. The composite shows enhanced supercapacitive and electrocatalytic performance with respect to the pristine NiAl2O4. The NiAl2O4/GQDs-modified gold electrode has been applied to develop an electrochemical method for glucose detection. The constructed biosensor allowed to sense glucose in serum of diabetes patients.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Diabetes mellitus (DM) is a worldwide spread disease, whose prevalence is ever increasing [1]. The United Nations and WHO identify it as one of the largest global public health concerns [2,3]. It prevails in the society since ancient history [4]. The rapid civilization advancement fosters the unhealthy diet and the sedentary lifestyle across the globe leading to the pandemic of overweight and obesity [5]. The latter are considered attributable risk factors for the global burden of DM [5,6]. It is anticipated thus that the number of people suffering from diabetes will increase since 2019 from 9.3 % up to 10.9 % of the population until 2045; i.e. 237 million more cases [7]. Untreated or late diagnosed DM leads to the life-threatening complications, i.a. kidney failure, liver disease, heart attack, stroke, diabetic retinopathy, to name a few [1,8]. The most promising strategy to eliminate those dangerous conditions is in early diagnosis and prompt administration of a medical treatment. This calls for development of fast and low-cost diagnostic methodologies.

Blood glucose concentration is directly related to DM. Therefore, it is of utmost importance to attentively monitor its level. Hitherto, several advanced techniques including high-performance liquid chromatography, capillary zone electrophoresis and calorimetry were employed in glucose sensing [9]. They, however, require advanced instrumentation, yielding high cost and thus are far from the use in realistic applications. The electrochemical methods emerge as attractive alternative. Most of them however rely on the glucose oxidase-modified electrodes, which demonstrate poor stability, high cost and complexity of fabrication. Therefore, non-enzymatic electrochemical sensors (NEESs) emerge as promising devices for determination of glucose and other markers [10]. NEESs offer higher electrochemical stability, better sensitivity and lower production costs with respect to the enzymatic sensors.

The electrode material is the key component of NEES; it provides sensor with high sensitivity and fast response. Metal oxides, and particularly mixed metal oxides are especially appealing for use in NEESs [10]. Spinel binary metal oxides (SBMOs) with the formula AB2O4 offer multifunctionality [11] and have already served as pigments, supercapacitors, oxygen carriers, hydrogen storage materials, and particularly as photo- [12] and electrocatalysts [13]. Undoubtly, their versatility is rising the scientific community attention. Additionally, the catalytic activity of SBMOs has been exploited in numerous reactions [14]; i.a. in oil [15] and methane reforming [16], hydrocarbons dehydrogenation [17], hydrodechlorination [18], acylation of alcohols, phenols and amines [19], to name a few.

Nickel aluminate (NiAl2O4) belongs to SBMOs; it contains earth-abundant elements and thus can be prepared at low cost via simple co-precipitation procedure [20]. Spinel aluminates are thermally and chemicaly stable [21,22]. They also show mechanical resistance and present low surface acidity [23]. NiAl2O4 contains Ni(II) ions, which could be oxidized to Ni(III) [24]. The latter feature renders NiAl2O4 with electrocatalytic potential, which has been already exploited in gallic acid detection in food samples [25]. Although, SBMOs offer easy and low-cost preparation, biocompatibility and robustness; they suffer from poor electrical conductivity, which is important for electrochemical applications. The electrode’s electrical conductivity can be boosted by introduction of additives, e.g. carbon nanostructures [26,27]. With this aim, carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [28], graphene (GR) [29], graphene oxide (GO) [30], and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) [31] have been blended with SBMOs. For instance, nickel-containing SBMOs, i.e. NiCo2O4, NiMn2O4, NiFe2O4, have been thus far combined with carbon nanotubes [28] and reduced graphene oxide and used in biosensing [31–33]. The electrocatalytic effect of such modified electrodes was scrutinized in human immune deficiency virus-1 gene [28], glucose [32,33], and ledipasvir [31] sensing.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) represent zero-dimensional sp2 bonded carbon atoms organized in a flat, honeycomb-like structure. They can be viewed as small fragments of graphene with diameter below 100 nm and typically 1–10 layers [34]. GQDs are attractive due to their size-dependent electro-optical properties, photoluminescence, exceptional thermal and electrical conductivity, good solubility, biocompatibility, and facile fabrication [34,35]. The wide span of GQDs advantageous characteristics led to their application in biosensing,[36] bioimaging [37], drug delivery [35], energy storage [38], electrocatalysis [38], photocatalysis [20,39], and water remediation [40].

Herein we present a synthetic route to afford nickel aluminate-graphene quantum dots (NiAl2O4/GQDs) composite for glucose sensing. The physicochemical characterization of the electrocatalytic material was performed by SEM and TEM microscopies, XPS and EDX spectroscopies, IR spectrometry and porosimetry. The electrochemical performance of composite was evaluated via cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) measurements. The glucose detection was validated through differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), while selectivity of the analyte’s detection was examined amperometrically. Finally, we utilized the developed biosensing method for glucose detection in serum samples donated by the University Clinical Hospital in Bialystok (Poland).

All chemicals were purchased from Sigma Aldrich: aluminium nitrate nonahydrate, ascorbic acid (AA), citric acid monohydrate (CA), dopamine (DA), glucose, nickel(II) nitrate hexahydrate, uric acid (UA). The chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further treatment. Aqueous ammonia (25 %) and sodium hydroxide were obtained from POCh (Gliwice, Poland).

NiAl2O4 nanoparticles were prepared according to the following procedure: Ni(NO3)·6 H2O (1 mmol) and Al(NO3)·9H2O (2 mmol) were dissolved in 100 mL of Milli-Q water. The aqueous ammonia (25 %) solution was added until reaching pH ~ 8. The formed precipitate was filtered, washed three times with water and dried in an oven at 100 °C for 6 h. The as-prepared product was annealed at 800 °C for 1 h to afford the crystalline NiAl2O4 spinel.

GQDs were prepared following the previously reported protocol [41]. The NiAl2O4/GQDs composite was synthesized by a facile method. The crystalline nickel aluminate (0.85 mmol) was introduced into the 50 mL of CA (0.62 mmol) aqueous solution. The as prepared suspension was taken to 200 °C under vigorous stirring. In the course of 30 min a transparent liquid turned amber. Then, the temperature was decreased to 140 °C, and 10 mL of deionized water were introduced. The heating was kept until affording a dry greenish powder.

Scanning electron microscopy images were recorded with secondary electron SEM using FEI INSPECT S50 scanning electron microscope; accelerating voltage of the electron beam of 25 keV and the working distance of 10 mm. TEM images were taken with a transmission electron microscope system Libra 120 (Zeiss, Germany).

Powdered NiAl2O4/GQDs was pressed onto transparent double sided tape, the excess residue was sprayed off with an argon gun. As prepared samples were examined with the XPS Versa Probe by Physical Electronics. The system uses the monochromatic Al Kα line (1486.7 eV) with a spot size of 100 µm. The samples are mounted and electrically isolated to have a floating potential. Electrical charging of the sample surface was neutralised with use of a combination of Ar+ ion and electron flux. First, wide-scan surveys of the whole energy region were performed, and subsequently high-resolution scans of the individual orbitals of interest including carbon, nickel and oxygen.
The FTIR spectra were registered with a Nicolet iN10 MX spectrometer (Thermo Scientific) at room temperature in the range from 400 to 4000 cm−1.

Nitrogen adsorption/desorption measurements were carried out using an ASAP2020 analyzer (Micromeritics Corp.). Prior measurements in liquid nitrogen, samples were degassed for 24 h at 130 °C in order to remove the moisture.

The PGSTAT 302 N (AUTOLAB) potentiostat was used for voltammetric and impedance measurements. The three-electrode system was used; Au (10 ×3 mm) served as working electrode, Pt mesh (0.25 mm) acted as the counter electrode and Ag/AgCl was employed as a reference electrode. Before starting the measurements, the surface of the working electrode was polished using very fine carborundum paper followed by 0.3 mm alumina and 0.25 mm diamond paste. The electrode was then sonicated in water to remove traces of alumina, washed with water, and dried. Finally, the surface of Au was modified by deposition of 15 μL of synthesized material solution (3 mg mL−1 in EtOH with the addition of conductive carbon paint) and solvent evaporation at room temperature. Galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) tests were performed with the identical electrode system at different current densities (0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 A g −1). All electrochemical measurements were taken from 0.1 M NaOH solution.

The specific capacitance from cyclic voltammetry (CV) ( Table 1) was calculated based on the equation:
(1)
C
p
=
∫
IdV
vm ∆ V
where C
p – specific capacitance [F g−1], V – potential [V], v – scan rate [V s−1], m – mass of active material [g], ∆V – potential window [V].

The GCD measurements allowed to calculate specific capacitance based on the following equation:
(2)
C
p
=
It
m ∆ V
where I – discharge current [A], t – discharge time, m – mass of active material [g], ∆V – potential window of discharge.

Four consecutive diabetes patients admitted to the 2nd Department of Nephrology in January 2020 and four healthy controls were recruited. Three females and one male, at the age of 37, 65, 28 and 77, respectively, were included; all with diabetes type 2, except for one patient with diabetes type 1 (P3). All patients were treated with low-carbohydrate diet and metformin with gliclazide (P1 and P2) or insulin (P3 and P4). The details of obtained glucose concentration for the patients and reference healthy controls are shown in Table 2. Healthy control group consisted of two females and two males at the age of 35, 74, 39 and 35, respectively.

Random glucose concentration was measured in patients with diabetes and in healthy controls. Serum samples were prepared by centrifugation at 3800 g for 30 min at room temperature within 30 min of collection, next aliquoted and stored at − 70 °C until further testing with sensor was performed. Additionally, blood samples from the same collection underwent prompt glucose concentration measurement in the hospital laboratory using standard enzymatic method with the hexokinase (Alinity Abbott).

The study protocol adhered to the principles of the Declaration of Helsinki and was approved by the local ethics committee (approval no. APK.002.16.2020). Written informed consent was obtained from all participants before data collection.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) allowed us to observe the morphology of the composite and its components. The pristine nickel aluminate forms aggregates with diameters in the range 2–13 nm ( Fig. 1a and Fig. S1b). In turn, GQDs appear as small dots of sizes in the range of 1–7 nm (Fig. 1b and Fig. S1a) [20]. Decoration of spinel with GQDs decreased the composite particles sizes (Fig. 1c and Fig. S1c). NiAl2O4/GQDs forms aggregates with particles diameters ranging between 2 and 10 nm. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern confirmed the presence of spinel structure in both NiAl2O4 (Fig. 1d) and the NiAl2O4/GQDs (Fig. 1e). The SAED pattern corresponds to the characteristic planes of the spinel nickel aluminate, i.e. (311), (400), and (440) [25].

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) allowed us to further characterize morphology and chemical composition of NiAl2O4/GQDs. The composite exhibits a rough surface with no regular organization (Fig. 1f). Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis demonstrated the presence of Ni, Al, O and C; those elements are uniformly distributed in the composite as evidenced by EDX mapping (Fig. 1g-j). For the in-depth elemental analysis we turned to X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. 1k-p). XPS spectra of the pristine nickel aluminate (Fig. 1k-m) and the composite (Fig. 1n-p) evidence the spectral evolution at all core levels. NiAl2O4 contains mainly adventitious carbon (Fig. 1k). The composite in turn shows a rise of OC–O species (Fig. 1n); a feature assigned to GQDs [42]. The Ni2p (Fig. 1m and Fig. 1p) spectra visualize Ni(II) species of the oxide and hydroxide origin. The oxide form with the energy maximizing at 856.23 eV dominates in NiAl2O4. Distribution of nickel species reverses in NiAl2O4/GQDs; the hydroxide form with the energy at 855.06 eV is major in the composite. We assign this behavior to the thermal treatment that pristine spinel is subjected to during the synthesis of NiAl2O4/GQDs. Both samples contain also a minimal amount of metallic Ni which was formed during calcination [43]; 1.9 % and 2.4 % for NiAl2O4 and NiAl2O4/GQDs, respectively (see ESI, Fig. S2). The O1s spectra of the pristine spinel (Fig. 1m) and the composite (Fig. 1p) are mainly composed of the aluminium (Al–O) and carbon bonded oxygen atoms. The contribution of the latter however is higher in NiAl2O4/GQDs, i.e. 28.5 %, with respect to pristine NiAl2O4 with 19.7 % of C–O and CO bonded oxygen atoms. This results from the presence of GQDS in the composite, thus GQDs possess the oxidized carbon atoms (i.e. C–O, C–O–C, CO, O–CO).

The IR spectrum of GQDs ( Fig. 2a) feature a broad band in the range 2200–3500 cm−1, which originates from the hydroxy and carboxy groups. The bands at 3489 cm−1 and 1687 cm−1 are attributed to the bending and stretching modes of the OH groups, respectively [25]. Additionally, the presence of CC, CH and CO bonds give rise to the signals at 1687, 1407, and 1282 cm−1, respectively [44]. The spectrum of the pristine spinel (Fig. 2a) has two major bands within 2500–3700 and 550–900 cm−1; they correspond to the hydroxy and carboxy groups and the metal-oxygen vibrational modes, respectively. In turn, IR spectrum of the NiAl2O4/GQDs composite (Fig. 2a) shows a broad band in the range 2500–3700 cm−1. This signal arises from the hydroxy and carboxy groups; the latter we also identified in the pristine spinel. The intense and rather broad band with a maximum at 1580 cm−1 comprises the CC and CO functionalities; in turn CH vibrational modes gave rise to the band at 1396 cm−1. The composite also presents characteristic bands at 527, 650, and 681 cm−1, which arise from the vibrational modes of NiO, AlO and NiOAl bonds, respectively [45].

The porosity of the synthesized materials was examined via nitrogen adsorption-desorption experiments. From those studies we derived the information on Brunaer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area, pore diameter, pore volume and nanoparticle size (see Table 1). GQDs exhibit an isotherm of a non-porous material (Fig. 2b, Fig. S3). In turn, NiAl2O4 and NiAl2O4/GQDs present typical type IV isotherm shapes (Fig. 2b). Pronounced rounded knee, that appears at lower relative pressures (< 0.05), points to the monolayer adsorption and the micropore filling. As the pressure ratios increase the mesopores are gradually filled; a feature corresponding to the multilayer adsorption. As typical to the type IV isotherm, in case of NiAl2O4 and NiAl2O4/GQDs, desorption curve takes a different course and gives rise to the hysteresis loop; the capillary condensation feature. The disk-like flat shape and the small size justifies the reasonably small surface area of GQDs particles; BET = 19 m2 g−1. Nevertheless, reported herein GQDs have 89 % higher BET with respect to GQDs reported elsewhere; BET = 10 m2 g−1 [36]. The modification of the post-treatment of the as-synthesized nickel aluminate, i.e. a decrease of calcination time from 3 h to 1 h, allowed us to increase the specific surface area of the pristine NiAl2O4 from 14 [12] to 226 m2 g−1. In result, the BET value for the composite is of 183 m2 g−1; GQDs contribute to the slight decrease of the surface area. The decoration of spinel particles with GQDs does not affect the pore diameter; both NiAl2O4 and NiAl2O4/GQDs present pore diameters of 6.45 nm. However, the pore volume of the pristine spinel is considerably higher, i.e. 0.57 cm3 g−1, with respect to the composite bearing a pore volume of 0.35 cm3 g−1 (Table 1, Fig. 2c).

The described synthesized materials were utilized for modification of gold electrodes. First, the bare Au electrode was covered by carbon paste layer (Au/CP). Afterwards, we independently deposited nickel aluminate, GQDs and the composite and thus we prepared Au/CP/NiAl2O4, Au/CP/GQDs and Au/CP/NiAl2O4/GQDs electrodes, respectively. Cyclic voltammograms (CVs) allowed us to characterize and compare the modified electrodes. The observed current increased in the following order Au/CP < Au/CP/NiAl2O4 < Au/CP/GQDs < Au/CP/NiAl2O4/GQDs ( Fig. 3a). The composite-modified electrode, Au/CP/NiAl2O4/GQDs, provided the highest current and thus also the highest specific capacitance. The latter increased from 42, to 46 and 114 F g−1 for NiAl2O4, GQDs, and NiAl2O4/GQDs, respectively. This order does not align with the specific surface areas of each electrode’s material. In fact, NiAl2O4 demonstrates the highest BET (226 m2 g−1), while its specific capacitance (42 F g−1) is of the same order of magnitude as the one exhibited by GQDs (46 F g−1). The best capacitance characteristic is shown by the composite-modified electrode with BET of 183 m2 g−1 and prominent specific capacitance of 114 F g−1.

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) allowed us to establish the influence of NiAl2O4 decoration with GQDs on the electrochemical process occurring at the surface of the electrode. The Nyquists plots (Fig. 3b) recorded in frequency range of 100 kHz – 0.1 Hz are characterized by the semicircles (Fig. 3c) and the inclined linear parts, which occur in the high- and low-frequency regions, respectively. The semicircles originate from the electron-transfer limited processes. The linear regions in turn are related to the diffusion-limited phenomena. We proposed the equivalent circuit (Fig. 3c), from which the charge transfer resistance values (RCT) were extracted. GQDs demonstrate the biggest slope of the straight tail among the three modified electrodes. Therefore, the GQDs-modified electrode shows the highest electrolyte diffusion rate. It has also the smallest charge transfer resistance (RCT) of 25 Ω. In turn, the NiAl2O4-modified electrode exhibits the highest RCT of 42 Ω. The decoration of the spinel structure with GQDs leads thus to the significant decrease of RCT for NiAl2O4/GQDs, 4 Ω. All in all, we demonstrated, that NiAl2O4/GQDs presents an improved electron transfer ability with respect to NiAl2O4.

The capacitive properties of the synthesized materials we further examined by the galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) measurements. The GCD curves of NiAl2O4/GQDs and NiAl2O4 have asymmetric triangular shapes (see Fig. 3d, Fig. S4). At current density of 1 A g−1 the specific capacitance of the NiAl2O4/GQDs composite was of 163 F g−1, while for NiAl2O4 it was considerably lower, of 59 F g−1, respectively. Depending on the current density the discharge time varied (see Fig. 3d) and thus the specific capacitance of the NiAl2O4/GQDs was between 163, 149, 129, 83 and 74 F g−1, for 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 A g−1, respectively (Fig. 3e). The improved specific capacity of the composite can be attributed to the synergistic behavior of NiAl2O4 and GQDs, i.a. i) high electrical conductivity of GQDs, ii) high specific surface area of NiAl2O4, and, iii) broad pore size distribution provided by spinel. Therefore, the composite exhibits over 10-fold lower charge transfer resistance with respect to the pristine spinel and thus an improved electron transfer ability.

The GCD studies allowed us to calculate the energy and power density for different current densities. Those parameters characterize the performance of supercapacitors. The power density for NiAl2O4/GQDs increases from 485, 987, 1283, 1651–1992 W kg−1 as the energy density decreases from 16.1, 15.4, 10, 6, to 5 Wh kg−1, respectively. The Ragone plot (Fig. 3f) represents the above mentioned energy density vs. power density relation. It comprises the results obtained for NiAl2O4/GQDs and pristine NiAl2O4. The energy density of the composite calculated at 2 A g−1 was of 5 Wh kg−1, i.e. 5-fold higher than the energy density of the pristine NiAl2O4 (1 Wh kg−1). Hence, the GQDs enrich the composite with better supercapacitor performance.

Finally, we attempted the electrochemical sensing of glucose. In order to select the best performing electrode the CV curves were recorded with the gold electrode modified only with the carbon paste, NiAl2O4 or NiAl2O4/GQDs in the presence of 1 mM glucose in 0.1 M NaOH (Fig. S5). The current corresponding to the potential of glucose oxidation, i.e. within the range 0.2 – 0.6 V [46–49], was the highest at the NiAl2O4/GQDs-modified electrode. Further DPV analysis confirmed that the glucose oxidation occurs at 0.37 V ( Fig. 4c). The observed behavior we assigned to the catalytic effect of Ni(II), high surface area, and low charge-transfer resistance of the composite. In order to identify the character of the electrochemical process occurring at the electrode’s surface we turned to scan rates tests. Fig. 4a shows the CV curves obtained at Au/CP/NiAl2O4/GQDs in 0.1 M NaOH containing glucose of 1 mM at different scan rates. The linear correlation of current response versus square root of scan rate (Fig. 4b) implies a diffusion-controlled glucose oxidation.

Based on the series of DPV curves registered at varying glucose concentrations (Fig. 4c) we obtained a linear calibration curve (see Fig. 4d). Table 2 includes the derived validation parameters. The constructed biosensor is characterized by the linear range 0.25 – 5.0 mM and limit of detection (LOD) of 0.06 mM, which are compatible with the glucose concentrations expected in the human blood serum. The RSD value calculated for the current response of 5 independent sensors used in the detection of 1 mM glucose was 6.3 % indicating an acceptable repeatability. The obtained parameters are comparable with those obtained for other glucose sensors constructed with nickel oxide-based electrodes (Table 2). The validation parameters reported herein also allow for glucose sensing in real samples, i.a. serum, plasma (see following section).

The selectivity of the constructed biosensor was tested via chronoamperometric experiments (see Fig. 4e). The Au/CP/NiAl2O4/GQDs exhibits a high electrochemical response upon addition of 2 mM of glucose. The subsequent addition of potential interfering agents, i.e. ascorbic acid (AA), dopamine (DA), and uric acid (UA), of 2 mM each, causes only negligible current responses. The significant current rise was observed after addition of the following three portions of glucose. Thereby, we demonstrated the selectivity of Au/CP/NiAl2O4/GQDs electrode towards glucose oxidation in presence of high concentrations of common blood interferents, e.g. AA, DA, UA.

Finally, we verified the practical application of the developed sensor by determining the glucose concentration in serum from diabetes patients and healthy people. The biosensor reported herein shows good correlation with reference method used in hospital ( Table 3). Glucose measurements are accurate with mean standard deviation from laboratory glucose concentration of only ± 2.4 % for study group and ± 6.9 % for healthy controls. Currently, the International Organization for Standardization (ISO), organ responsible for defining the standards for blood glucose meters, requires accuracy within ± 20 % for new blood glucose measurement methods. Hence, presented results are beyond the required obligations.

Decoration of NiAl2O4 with GQDs significantly improves physicochemical features of the NiAl2O4/GQDs and thus makes it an alluring electrocatalyst with respect to the pristine double-metal oxide. The introduced composite not only has high surface area (183 m2 g−1), but also decreased charge-transfer resistance and improved specific capacitance all of which desirable characteristics expediting ultrasensitive biosensing platforms. Finally, the NiAl2O4/GQDs-modified electrode facilitates enhanced sensitivity towards glucose enabling its electrochemical sensing directly in human serum.

Elzbieta Regulska: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft, review & editing. Joanna Breczko: Investigation – Electrochemical studies. Anna Basa: Investigation – TEM measurements. Beata Szydlowska: Investigation - XPS analysis. Katarzyna Kakareko, Alicja Rydzewska-Rosołowska, Tomasz Hryszko: Biological material collection & glucose sensing with reference method

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Polish Ministry of Science and Higher Education under subsidy for maintaining the research potential of the Faculty of Chemistry, Polish Ministry of Science and Higher Education. Diffractometer, IR spectrometer, SEM and TEM microscopes, porosimeter and potentiostat were funded by EU, as part of the Operational Programme Development of Eastern Poland, projects Nr POPW.01.03.00-20-034/09-00 and POPW.01.03.00-20-004/11-00.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Ma, M. Yang, C. Li, H. Tan, L. Deng, S. Xie, F. Xu, L. Wang, Y. Song, Preparation of spinel nickel-cobalt oxide nanowrinkles/reduced graphene oxide hybrid for nonenzymatic glucose detection at physiological level, Electrochimica Acta. 220 (2016) 545–553. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2016.10.163.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly improved electrooxidation of glucose at a nickel(II) oxide/multi-walled carbon nanotube modified glassy carbon electrode
Bioelectrochemistry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Shamsipur, M. Najafi, M.-R.M. Hosseini, Highly improved electrooxidation of glucose at a nickel(II) oxide/multi-walled carbon nanotube modified glassy carbon electrode, Bioelectrochemistry. 77 (2010) 120–124. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bioelechem.2009.07.007.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sugars electrooxidation at glassy carbon electrode decorate with multi-walled carbon nanotubes with nickel oxyhydroxide
Int. J. Electrochem. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.S. Acelino, L.P. Leonardo, R.S. Nelson, Sugars Electrooxidation at Glassy Carbon Electrode Decorate with Multi-Walled Carbon Nanotubes with Nickel OxyHydroxide, Int J Electrochem Sci. 9 (2014) 7746–7762.
